titleStmt.title: Large-Signal and High-Frequency Analysis of Nonuniformly Doped or Shaped PN-Junction Diodes *

analytic.title: Large-Signal and High-Frequency Analysis of Nonuniformly Doped or Shaped PN-Junction Diodes *

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: An analytical theory of nonuniformly doped or shaped PN-junction diodes submitted to largesignals at high frequencies is presented. The resulting expressions can be useful to evaluate the performance of semiconductor device modeling software. The transverse averaging technique is employed to reduce the three-dimensional charge carrier transport equations into the quasi-onedimensional form, with all physical quantities averaged out over the longitudinally-varying cross section. Although, it is assumed an axial symmetry, this approach gives rise to useful analytic expressions for the static current-voltage characteristics, the diffusion conductance, and diffusion capacitance as a function of the signal amplitude and the cross section non-uniformity.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Depending on the application, different levels of device modeling are used to predict the behavior of electronic circuits. For circuits with many devices, compact models are required to reduce the simulation time. For circuit building blocks with a few devices, physical models may be used to get further insight. Physics-based modeling requires more computer resources, but it has the advantage of being valid at a wider operating range, and offers easier to interpret parameters [1]. When modeling a PN-junction, different operating conditions are possible, such as: steady-state small-signal, steady-state large-sginal, DC, and transient [1], [2], [3], [4].

The transverse averaging technique (TAT) allows the general three-dimensional (3D) equations of semiconductor electronics to be converted into the so-called quasi-one-dimensional (quasi-1D) form. The quasi-1D equations involve all the physical scalar quantities (potential, charge density, etc.) and longitudinal components of the vector quantities (electric field, current density, etc.) in the form averaged over the longitudinally-varying cross section S(z) and dependent only on the longitudinal coordinate z [5]. It was first applied to derive analytical expressions for the depletion capacitance of the PN-junctions with nonuniform doping impurity profile and cross-sectional geometry peculiar to various real devices. It may also be useful to P-i-N diode modeling [7]. This work does not apply to avalanche type diodes, such as IMPATT, as no generation/ionization process is included in the theory at this point [8].

Besides, the average one-dimensional equations also include the contour integrals along interface lines which take into account the proper boundary conditions between different domains of the semiconductor structure. Such equations are completely equivalent to the initial three-dimensional equations and in this respect are accurate except that they deal with physical quantities averaged over the cross section of a nonuniform semiconductor structure.

This paper is devoted to the generalization of the large-signal and high-frequency theory of the charge carrier transport developed previously for uniform PN-junctions [5], [9], [6] to nonuniform structures. Application of the general TAT relations given in paper [5] to deriving the quasi-1D drift-diffusion equations for nonuniform junctions is performed in Sec. II. Spectral solution of the quasi-1D diffusion equations for nonuniform PN-junctions is set forth in Sec. III. Section IV deals with the derivation and analysis of the external circuit current, which serves as a basis for obtaining the static current-voltage characteristics (Sec. V) and the dynamic impedance (Sec. VI) of the PN-junction diodes with nonuniform cross section.

div.head: II. DERIVATION OF QUASI-ONE-DIMENSIONAL DRIFT-DIFFUSION EQUATIONS FOR NONUNIFORM PN-JUNCTIONS
div.p: The initial equations for derivation of the quasi-1D drift-diffusion equations of the nonuniform PN-junctions are the three-dimensional continuity equations and the current density expressions [8]:
div.formula: • for holes ∂p ∂t + 1 q ∇ • j p = - p -p n τ p ,(1)
j p = qpv p -q∇(D p p), v p = µ p (E)E;
div.p: • for electrons
div.formula: ∂n ∂t - 1 q ∇ • j n = - n -n p τ n ,(2)
j n = qnv n + q∇(D n n), v n = µ n (E)E .
div.p: with the normal components of currents that obey the following surface-recombination boundary conditions [8]:
div.formula: n • j p L = qR ps , R ps = s p (p s -p n ),(3)
div.p: and
div.formula: n • j n L = qR ns , R ns = s n (n s -n p ). (4
div.p: Here p n , n p and p, n are the equilibrium and nonequilibrium densities of minority carriers in the n-and p-regions, D p(n) , µ p(n) and τ p(n) are the diffusion constant, mobility, and lifetime for holes (electrons). Integration of Eqs. ( 1) and (2) over the cross section area S(z) by using the TAT relations [5] produces the linear integrals along the contour L(z) bounding S(z). For holes, calculating the average and applying the 3-D extension of Green's Theorem in (Eq. 1), yields
div.formula: ∂(pS) ∂t + 1 q ∂( jpz S) ∂z + 1 q L(z) n • j p cos θ dl = - p -p n τ p S,(5)
1 q jpz S = pμ p Ēz S - ∂( Dp pS) ∂z - L(z) n • e z cos θ D p p dl.(6)
div.p: Here p s , n s are the nonequilibrium minority-carrier volume densities taken at surface points of the structure, and s p(n) is the surface recombination velocity for holes (electrons).
Here the average quantities Ēz , jpz , and p are introduced [5], while the average mobility and diffusion constant for holes (and similar ones for electrons) are defined as
div.formula: μp = pv p (E)E z /E p Ēz and Dp = D p p p .
div.p: For axially-symmetrical structures with S(z) = πa 2 (z), we have
div.formula: n • e z cos θ = -tan θ = - da(z) dz ,
div.p: so that the contour integrals in Eqs. ( 5) and ( 6) with the boundary condition (3) assume the form
div.formula: 1 q L(z) n • j p cos θ dl = 2s p 1 + (da/dz) 2 a(z) (p s -p n )S,(7)
div.p: By substituting expressions ( 6)-( 8) into Eq. ( 5) and introducing the effective lifetime τp to allow for both the volume and surface recombination
Similarly, from the initial equations ( 2) and ( 4) we obtain the quasi-1D drift-diffusion equation for electrons injected into the p-region:
div.formula: ∂ ∂t (nS) + ∂ ∂z (nμ n Ēz S) - ∂ 2 ∂z 2 ( Dn nS) + ∂ ∂z D ns n s dS dz = - n -n p τn S.(10)
div.p: All the average quantities μp(n) , Dp(n) , and τp(n) will be considered as phenomenologically given parameters with dropping the bar sign over them for simplicity.

Quasi-one-dimensional equations ( 9) and ( 10) are of the general form applicable for both the P iN -and PN-diodes. For low level of injection in the PN-diodes we can assume Ēz = 0 for diode base so that Eqs. ( 9) and (10) take the simplified form:
div.formula: ∂ ∂t (pS) - ∂ ∂z S ∂ ∂z (D p p) - ∂ ∂z (D p p -D ps p s ) dS dz = - p -p n τ p ,(11)
∂ ∂t (nS) - ∂ ∂z S ∂ ∂z (D n n) - ∂ ∂z (D n n -D ns n s ) dS dz = - n -n p τ n . (12
div.p: Not having a specific knowledge of surface properties, we shall assume that D ps p s = D p p and D ns n s = D n n. Then, from equations (11) and (12) follows the quasi-one-dimensional diffusion equations for the excess concentrations of holes, ∆p(z, t) = p(z, t) -p n , and electrons, ∆n(z, t) = n(z, t) -n p , injected into the appropriate neutral parts of the PN-diode [5]:
where
div.formula: L p(n) = D p(n) τ p(n)
div.p: is the diffusion length for holes (electrons). The additional last term involving ∂ ln S/∂z ≡ S ′ (z)/S(z) takes into account the cross-sectional nonuniformity.

div.head: III. SPECTRAL SOLUTION OF QUASI-ONE-DIMENSIONAL DIFFUSION EQUATIONS
div.p: In general case, the voltage applied to the PN-junction consists of the DC bias voltage V 0 and the AC harmonic signal V ∼ cos ωt:
div.formula: v(t) = V 0 + V ∼ cos ωt ≡ V 0 + V 1 (ω)e iωt + c.c. .(15)
div.p: Nonlinearity of electronic processes in the PN-junction produces the frequency harmonics kω so that solutions of Eqs. ( 13) and ( 14) have the form of Fourier series:
div.formula: ∆p(z, t) = ∞ k =-∞ ∆p k (z) e ikωt ,(16) ∆n
(z, t) = ∞ k =-∞ ∆n k (z) e ikωt . (17
div.p: Real values of ∆p(z, t) and ∆n(z, t) are provided with the following relations for the complex amplitudes: ∆p k = ∆p * -k and ∆n k = ∆n * -k . Substitution of the required solutions ( 16) and (17) into Eqs. ( 13) and ( 14) with regard for the orthogonality of harmonics reduces to the following equations for the harmonic amplitudes [5]:
div.formula: d 2 ∆p k dz 2 + d ln S dz d∆p k dz - ∆p k L 2 pk = 0, (18
d 2 ∆n k dz 2 + d ln S dz d∆n k dz - ∆n k L 2 nk = 0,(19)
div.p: where
div.formula: L pk = L p 1 + ikωτ p and L nk = L n √ 1 + ikωτ n .
div.p: As an analytical approximation to a mesa-like structure, the special case of exponential change of the cross section S(z) = S 0 exp(2αz) is considered, Eqs. ( 18) and ( 19) turn into the linear equations [5]
div.formula: d 2 ∆p k dz 2 + 2α d∆p k dz - ∆p k L 2 pk = 0,(20)
d 2 ∆n k dz 2 + 2α d∆n k dz - ∆n k L 2 nk = 0. (21
div.p: Representing the desired solution in the form of e -λz , we obtain the following characteristic equation for Eqs. ( 20) and ( 21):
div.formula: λ 2 -2αλ -L -2 k = 0 , where L k = L pk for holes, L nk for electrons. (22
div.p: The complex roots of Eq. ( 22) can be written as follows
div.formula: λ 1,2 = ± Λ ± pk /L p for the n-region (z ≥ W n ), ± Λ ± nk /L n for the p-region (z ≤ -W p ),(23)
div.p: where we have introduced the following notations:
• for holes injected into the n-region
div.formula: (z ≥ W n ) Λ ± pk = (a α pk A α p ± αL p ) + ib α pk A α p , A α p = 1 + (αL p ) 2 , a α pk = 1 √ 2 1 + Θ α pk , b α pk = kωτ α p 2a α pk ,(24)
Θ α pk = 1 + (kωτ α p ) 2 , τ α p = τ p 1 + (αL p ) 2 ;
div.p: • for electrons injected into the p-region (z ≤ -W p )
div.formula: Λ ± nk = (a α nk A α n ± αL n ) + ib α nk A α n , A α n = 1 + (αL n ) 2 , a α nk = 1 √ 2 1 + Θ α nk , b α nk = kωτ α n 2a α nk ,(25)
Θ α nk = 1 + (kωτ α n ) 2 , τ α n = τ n 1 + (αL n ) 2 .
div.p: The general solutions of equations ( 20) and ( 21) have the following form:
div.formula: • for holes injected into the n-region (z ≥ W n ) ∆p k (z) = C + pk exp -Λ + pk z -W n L p + C - pk exp Λ - pk z -W n L p ;(26)
div.p: • for electrons injected into the p-region
div.formula: (z ≤ -W p ) ∆n k (z) = C + nk exp -Λ + nk z + W p L n + C - nk exp Λ - nk z + W p L n .(27)
div.p: In accordance with notations (24) and (25), always Re Λ ± pk = a α pk A α p ± αL p > 0 and Re Λ ± nk = a α nk A α n ± αL n > 0 so that for the PN-diodes with thick bases we can assume C - pk = 0 and C + nk = 0, which eliminates the necessity for boundary conditions on ohmic contacts. Taking into account formulas ( 26) and ( 27) with C - pk = C + nk = 0, the general solutions ( 16) and (17) of Eqs. ( 13) and ( 14) are written in the form
div.formula: ∆p(z, t) = ∞ k =-∞ C + pk exp -Λ + pk z -W n L p e ikωt ,(28) ∆n
(z, t) = ∞ k =-∞ C - nk exp Λ - nk z + W p L n e ikωt . (29
div.p: These expressions provide ∆p(z, t) → 0 as z → ∞ and ∆n(z, t) → 0 as z → -∞ because of Re Λ + pk > 0 and Re Λ - nk > 0. The constants C + pk and C - nk appearing in Eqs. ( 28) and ( 29) can be found from the conventional injection boundary conditions [8]:
div.formula: ∆p(W n , t) = p n f (t) for z = W n ,(30) ∆n
(-W p , t) = n p f (t) for z = -W p ,(31)
div.p: where for the applied voltage v(t) of the form (15) we have introduced the function
div.formula: f (t) = exp qv(t) κT -1. (32
div.p: Substitution of expressions ( 28) and (29) into the boundary conditions ( 30) and (31) yields
div.formula: ∞ k =-∞ C + pk e ikωt = p n f (t), (33
) ∞ k =-∞ C - nk e ikωt = n p f (t).
div.p: By using the orthogonality property of harmonics in expansions (33) it is easy to get the desired constants
div.formula: C + pk = p n F k and C - nk = n p F k ,(34)
div.p: where F k is the Fourier amplitude of the kth harmonic for the function f (t) given by formula (32), that is
div.formula: F k = 1 2π π -π f (t)e -ikωt dωt. (35
div.p: Substitution of the function (32) into formula (35) gives
div.formula: F 0 = I 0 (βV ∼ ) exp(βV 0 ) -1 for k = 0,(36)
F k = F -k = I k (βV ∼ ) exp(βV 0 ) for k = 0, (37
div.p: where we have used the modified Bessel functions of the first kind of order k (k = 0, ±1, ±2, . . .) having the following integral representation (see formula 8.431.5 in Ref. [10]):
div.formula: I k (βV ∼ ) = 1 π π 0 e βV∼ cos ωt cos kωt dωt. (38
div.p: This function depends on βV ∼ , where V ∼ is an amplitude of the signal applied to the PN-junction and β = q/κT .
Thus, with allowing for Eq. (34) the general solutions (28) and (29) of the diffusion equations ( 13) and ( 14) take the final form of spectral expansions:
div.formula: ∆p(z, t) = p n ∞ k =-∞ F k exp -Λ + pk z -W n L p e ikωt ,(39) ∆n
(z, t) = n p ∞ k =-∞ F k exp Λ - nk z + W p L n e ikωt . (40
div.p: These expressions allow us to obtain the spectral composition of the current flowing through the external circuit connected to the PN-diode.

div.head: IV. EXTERNAL CIRCUIT CURRENT FOR SEMICONDUCTOR DIODE WITH NONUNIFORM CROSS SECTION
div.p: The initial equation to derive an expression for the diode current is the law of total current conservation:
following from Maxwell's equation ∇× H = j p + j n + ǫ ∂E/∂t. The transverse averaging technique applied to Eq. ( 41) gives
div.formula: ∂ ∂z jpz + jnz + ǫ ∂ Ēz ∂t S + L(z) n • j cos θ dl + ∂ ∂t L(z) n • ǫE cos θ dl = 0. (42
div.p: The similar equation outside the semiconductor, where currents are absent and ∇ • (ǫ o E o ) = 0, has the following form:
div.formula: ∂ ∂z ǫ o ∂ Ēo z ∂t S o - ∂ ∂t L(z) n • ǫ o E o cos θ dl = 0, (43
div.p: where S o is an effective localization area of the fringe outside field E o such that usually S o ≪ S and ǫ o < ǫ. The addition of Eqs. ( 42) and (43) gives
div.formula: ∂ ∂z jpz + jnz S + ∂ ∂t ǫ Ēz S + ǫ o Ēo z S o + L(z) n • j cos θ dl + ∂ ∂t L(z) n • (ǫE -ǫ o E o ) cos θ dl = 0. (44
div.p: If a semiconductor surface contains traps with the charge density ρ s , there are the following boundary conditions on the contour L(z) [11]:
div.formula: n • ǫ o E o = n • ǫ E + ρ s and ∂ρ s ∂t = n • j .(45)
div.p: In this case, two contour integrals in Eq. ( 44) cancel each other and with allowing for inequality
div.formula: |ǫ o Ēo z S o | ≪ |ǫ Ēz S| formula (44) takes the form ∂ ∂z jpz (z, t) + jnz (z, t) + ǫ ∂ Ēz (z, t) ∂t S(z) = 0. (46
div.p: The quantity in brackets of Eq. ( 46), being independent of z, defines the external circuit current equal to
div.formula: J(t) = q(µ p p + µ n n) Ēz S -q D p ∂ p ∂z -D n ∂ n ∂z S + ǫ ∂ Ēz ∂t S.(47)
div.p: Here we have used expressions ( 6) and ( 8) for the average hole current jpz S and the similar expressions for the average electron current jnz S (with dropping the bar sign over μp(n) and Dp(n) ). All the terms on the right of Eq. (47) depend on both z and t but taken together at any cross section S(z) they yield the external circuit current J(t) as a function of only time.

Restricting our consideration to the PN-diodes with low injection, we can assume Ēz = 0 in neutral parts of the pand n-regions [8]. Then the external circuit current (47) is determined only by the averaged diffusion currents taken at any cross section S(z), for example, at z = W n :
div.formula: J(t) = -qD p ∂ p(z, t) ∂z z=Wn S(W n ) + qD n ∂ n(z, t) ∂z z=Wn S(W n ) ≡ jpz (W n , t)S(W n ) + jnz (W n , t)S(W n ). (48
div.p: Neglecting recombination processes inside the PN-junction, which is true if
div.formula: W = W n + W p ≪ L p and L n [8], we can write jnz (W n , t)S(W n ) = jnz (-W p , t)S(-W p ). (49
div.p: Substitution of relation (49) into Eq. ( 48) gives the external circuit current (cf. Eq. (31) in Ref. [5])
div.formula: J(t) = jpz (W n , t)S(W n ) + jnz (-W p , t)S(-W p ) = -qD p ∂∆p(z, t) ∂z z = Wn S(W n ) + qD n ∂∆n(z, t) ∂z z = -Wp S(-W p ),(50)
div.p: where ∆p = p -p n and ∆n = n -n p are the excess concentrations of injected carriers determined by formulas (39) and (40). Inserting these formulas into expression (50), we finally obtain the spectral representation for the external circuit current:
div.formula: J(t) = J s,p ∞ k =-∞ F k Λ + pk e ikωt + J s,n ∞ k =-∞ F k Λ - nk e ikωt .(51)
div.p: Here, the hole and electron contributions into the saturation current of a thick PN-junction are defined, as it is generally accepted [8], in the form
div.formula: J s,p = qD p p n L p S n and J s,n = qD n n p L n S p ,(52)
div.p: where
div.formula: S n ≡ S(W n ) = S 0 exp(2αW n ), S p ≡ S(-W p ) = S 0 exp(-2αW p ).
div.p: Expression (51) contains all the spectral components of the external circuit current including the DC current for k = 0 and the AC current for k = ± 1 which are of most interest for our further consideration.

div.head: V. STATIC CURRENT-VOLTAGE CHARACTERISTIC OF PN-DIODE WITH NONUNIFORM CROSS SECTION
div.p: The term in series (51) numbered by k = 0 corresponds to the DC current J 0 , which by using (36) can be written in the form of the static current-voltage characteristic:
Here we have introduced the saturation current for a nonuniform diode
div.formula: J s = J s,p r α p0 + J s,n r α n0 (54)
div.p: and, in accordance with expressions (24) and (25) for k = 0, have used the following notation:
div.formula: r α p0 ≡ Λ + p0 = A α p + αL p = 1 + (αL p ) 2 + αL p ,(55)
r α n0 ≡ Λ - n0 = A α n -αL n = 1 + (αL n ) 2 -αL n .
div.p: For the uniform p -n-junction (with α = 0) we have r α p0 = r α n0 = 1 and S n = S p = S 0 so that the static current-voltage characteristic retains the form (53) with the usual saturation current
div.formula: J s = J 0 s,p + J 0 s,n ≡ qD p p n L p S 0 + qD n n p L n S 0 ,(56)
div.p: where as before superscript 0 marks the cross-sectional uniformity (when α = 0 and S 0 = constant).

The modified Bessel function I 0 (βV ∼ ) appearing in Eq. ( 53) is completely the same as that obtained in our paper [5] and it distinguishes our expression (53) from the similar formula given in the known literature on semiconductor electronics [8]. Coincidence between them occurs only for such small signals that V ∼ ≪ κT /q and I 0 (βV ∼ ) ≃ 1. The function I 0 (βV ∼ ) ≥ 1 reflects the effect of signal rectification, which provides the contribution into the DC current from a signal and results in upward shifts of curves J 0 (V 0 ) with increasing the signal amplitude V ∼ .

div.head: VI. DYNAMIC IMPEDANCE OF PN-DIODE WITH NONUNIFORM CROSS SECTION
div.p: The first harmonic of the external current in the general expression (51) corresponds to terms numbered by k = ± 1 and equals
div.formula: J 1 (t) = J 1 (ω)e iωt + c.c.,(57)
div.p: where
div.formula: J 1 (ω) = F 1 J s,p Λ + p1 (ω) + J s,n Λ - n1(
div.p: ω) . The quantities Λ + p1 , Λ - n1 , and F 1 are respectively defined by formulas (24), (25), and (37) for k = 1.
Expressions (15) with V ∼ = 2V 1 (ω) and (57) allow one to introduce the dynamic admittance of the PN-diode as a function of frequency:
div.formula: Y (ω) = J 1 (ω) V 1 (ω) ≡ G d (ω) + iωC d (ω). (58
div.p: The dynamic (diffusion) conductance G d and the dynamic (diffusion) capacitance C d are defined in a customary way [8]. After substituting (57) into Eq. ( 58) and some transformations with regard for (54), we obtain
div.formula: G d = gG 0 J s,p r α p1 + J s,n r α n1 J s,p r α p0 + J s,n r α n0 ≡ gG 0 r α p1 J s,p J s + r α n1 J s,n J s ,(59)
C d = gG 0 ω J s,p q α p1 + J s,n q α n1 J s,p r α p0 + J s,n r α n0 ≡ gG 0 2 q α p1 J s,p τ p J s + q α n1 J s,n τ n J s . (60
div.p: Here we have introduced the new quantities:
div.formula: r α p1 = a α p1 A α p + αL p , r α n1 = a α n1 A α n -αL n ,(61)
q α p1 = 1 a α p1 A α p , q α n1 = 1 a α p1 A α p ,(62)
div.p: and, in accordance with Eqs. ( 24) and (25) for k = 1, used the following notation:
div.formula: ) Θ α p1 = 1 + (ωτ α p ) 2 , Θ α n1 = 1 + (ωτ α n ) 2 .
div.p: Formulas ( 59) and (60) contain the differential conductance G 0 of the static current-voltage characteristic J 0 (V 0 , V ∼ ) defined as
For the static characteristic of form (53), the differential conductance (64) depends on both the bias voltage V 0 and the signal amplitude V ∼ :
div.formula: G 0 (V 0 , V ∼ ) = J 0 + J s κT /q ≡ J s exp(βV 0 ) κT /q I 0 (βV ∼ ).(65)
div.p: Formulas ( 59) and (60) involve a quantity

composed of the modified Bessel functions I 0 and I 1 and introduced before in paper [5]. It depends on the signal amplitude V ∼ , so that g(V ∼ ) ≃ 1 for small signals when V ∼ ≪ κT /q and g(V ∼ ) → 0 as a function 2/(βV ∼ ) when V ∼ → ∞.
From Eqs. ( 65) and (66) it follows that
div.formula: gG 0 = g 1 J s exp(βV 0 ) κT /q ≡ I 1 (βV ∼ ) βV ∼ /2 J s exp(βV 0 ) κT /q . (67
div.p: After substituting (67) into Eqs. ( 59) and (60), they assume the following form:
div.formula: G d (ω) = g 1 G d0 r α p1 (ω) J s,p J s + r α n1 (ω) J s,n J s ,(68)
C d (ω) = g 1 C d0 q α p1(ω)
Q p Q + q α n1 (ω) Q n Q ,(69)
div.p: where, following to paper [5], we have introduced the charges Q p ≡ J s,p τ p = qL p p n S n , Q n ≡ J s,n τ n = qL n n p S p , and similar to Eq. ( 54)
div.formula: Q = Q p r α p0 + Q n r α n0 .(70)
div.p: The quantities G d0 and C d0 appearing in Eqs. ( 68) and (69) are defined, by analogy with those in paper [5], as
div.formula: G d0 (V 0 ) = J s exp(βV 0 ) κT /q = J s,p r α p0 + J s,n r α n0 κT /q e βV0 ≡ q κT qp n D p L p S n r α p0 + qn p D n L n S p r α n0 e qV0/κT ,(71)
C d0 (V 0 ) = Q exp(βV 0 ) 2κT /q = Q p r α p0 + Q n r α n0 2κT /q e βV0 ≡ q κT qp n L p 2 S n r α p0 + qn p L n 2 S p r α n0 e qV0/κT .(72)
div.p: An essential simplification of Eqs. (68)-( 69) and ( 71)-(72) occurs in the case of the one-sided P + N -junction with highly doped emitter when p n ≫ n p , W p ≪ W n , J s,n ≪ J s,p , Q n ≪ Q p , so that Eqs. (54) and (70) yield J s ≃ J s,p r α p0 and Q ≃ Q p r α p0 . Then, the quantities (71) and (72) can be written in the simplified form:
div.formula: G d0 (V 0 ) ≃ G 0 d0 (V 0 )r α p0 e 2αWn ,(73)
C d0 (V 0 ) ≃ C 0 d0 (V 0 )r α p0 e 2αWn ,(74)
div.p: where the newly introduced quantities (marked with superscript 0)
div.formula: G 0 d0 (V 0 ) = J 0 s,p exp(βV 0 ) κT /q ≡ qS 0 κT qp n D p L p e qV0/κT ,(75)
C 0 d0 (V 0 ) = Q 0 p exp(βV 0 ) 2κT /q ≡ qS 0 κT qp n L p 2 e qV0/κT ,(76)
div.p: correspond, as before, to the cross-sectionally uniform structures (with α = 0 and S 0 = constant). Substitution of Eqs. ( 73) and (74) into expressions (68) and (69) with J s,n ≃ 0 and Q n ≃ 0 converts them into the form appropriate to the one-sided junction:
div.formula: G d (ω, V ∼ ) G 0 d0 (V 0 ) ≃ F α G (ω) I 1 (βV ∼ ) βV ∼ /2 ,(77)
C d (ω, V ∼ ) C 0 d0 (V 0 ) ≃ F α C (ω) I 1 (βV ∼ ) βV ∼ /2 . (78
div.p: Here we have defined the frequency-dependent factors:
div.formula: F α G (ω) = r α p1 (ω) e 2αWn ≡ a α p1 (ω)A α p + αL p e 2αWn ,(79)
F α C (ω) = q α p1 (ω) e 2αWn ≡ 1 a α p1 (ω)A α p e 2αWn ,(80)
div.p: where
div.formula: A α p = 1 + (αL p ) 2 and a α p1 (ω)A α p = 1 √ 2 1+(αL p ) 2 + 1+(αL p ) 2 2 + (ωτ p ) 2 .
div.p: For the uniform PN-junction (with α = 0) from Eqs. (79) and (80) it follows that
so that expressions (77) and ( 78) take a form identical to formulas (48) and (49) obtained in paper [5].

The dependence on the signal amplitude V ∼ for the diffusion conductance G d (V ∼ ) and capacitance C d (V ∼ ) appearing in Eqs. ( 77)-(78) (as well as in the general formulas (68)-( 69)) is expressed by the function g 1 (V ∼ ) = I 1 (βV ∼ )/(βV ∼ /2).

The frequency dependence of the diffusion conductance G d (ω) and capacitance C d (ω) is produced by the functions r α p1 (ω) and q α p1 (ω). These functions are defined by Eqs. ( 61)-(63) to appear in the factors F α G (ω) and F α C (ω) given by Eqs. ( 79) and (80). The curves r α p1 (ω) and q α p1 (ω) are plotted in Fig. 1 for different values of the nonuniformity parameter αL p = 0, ±0, 5, ±1, ±2, ±3.

Two curves 1 corresponding to the uniform junction (αL p = 0) are fully the same as those shown in Fig. 23 of Chapter 2 in a book by Sze [8]. The cross-sectional nonuniformity (αL p = 0) changes the curves r α p1 (ω) and q α p1 (ω) in different ways. The function q α p1 (ω) < 1 always and it decreases with growing |αL p | regardless of the sign of αL p , as shown by solid curves 2(2a), 3(3a), 4(4a), 5(5a) in Fig. 1. By contrast, the function r α p1 (ω) increases for α > 0 (solid curves 2, 3, 4, 5) and decreases for α < 0 (dashed curves 2a, 3a, 4a, 5a) with growing |αL p |, as compared to curve 1 (for α = 0).
At low frequencies such that ωτ p ≪ 1 and a α p1 (ω) ≃ a α p1 (0) = 1, the functions r α p1 (ω) and q α p1 (ω) take constant values
div.formula: r α p1 (ω) ≃ r α p1 (0) = A α p + αL p and q α p1 (ω) ≃ q α p1 (0) = 1 A α p .
div.p: FIG. 2: Frequency dependencies of the quantities r α p1 (ω) and q α p1 (ω) for different values of the nonuniformity parameter αLp = 0 (solid curves 1), 0.5 (solid curves 2), 1 (solid curves 3), 2 (solid curves 4), 3 (solid curves 5); -0.5 (dashed curve 2a), -1 (dashed curve 3a), -2 (dashed curve 4a), -3 (dashed curve 5a).
Then, the factors (79) and (80) become frequency-independent and equal to
div.formula: F α G (ω) ≃ F α G (0) = r α p1(
F α C (ω) ≃ F α C (0) = q α p1 (0) e 2αWn ≡ 1 A α p e 2αLp(Wn/Lp) . ((83)
div.p: The dependence of the low-frequency factors (83) and (84) on the nonuniformity parameter αL p is shown in Fig. 2 for three ratios W n /L p = 0, 0.05, 0.1. Such small values of W n /L p are chosen to ensure the condition W n ≪ L p for neglecting recombination processes in the depletion layer of width W n [8]. Character of the cross-sectional nonuniformity (for α < 0 with S p > S n and for α > 0 with S p < S n ) exerts different influence on
div.formula: F α G (0) (or the conductance G d (0, V ∼ ) = g 1 (V ∼ )F α G (0) G 0 d0 ) and on F α C (0) (or the capacitance C d (0, V ∼ ) = g 1 (V ∼ )F α C (0) C 0 d0
div.p: ). The dashed curves in Fig. 2 demonstrate that the low-frequency capacitance C d (0, V ∼ ) for nonuniform structures (with α = 0) is always less than C 0 d0 for uniform ones (with α = 0) for both signs α > 0 and α < 0. But the low-frequency conductance G d (0, V ∼ ) depicted by solid curves increases for α > 0 (when S n > S p ) and decreases for α < 0 (when S n < S p ).

In conclusion, it is pertinent to note that the initial differential equations ( 18) and ( 19) have been solved by using the exponential approximation S(z) = S 0 exp(2αz) for the cross-sectional z-dependence. In this case, the sought eigenfunctions are of exponential form exp(±Λ ± pk z/L p ) and exp(±Λ ± nk z/L n ) to yield the general solutions (26) and (27). If the power approximation S(z) = S 0 (αz) 2m is more suitable, Eqs. ( 18) and (19) can be reduced to the following form (with u(z) = ∆p k (z) or ∆n k (z)):
div.formula: d 2 u(z) dz 2 - 2ν z du(z) dz -c 2 u(z) = 0,
div.p: whose solution is (see formula 8.494.9 in Ref. [10]) where Z ν+1/2 is the Bessel function of the first or second kind. For equations ( 18) and (19) we have ν = -m and c 2 = L -2 k , where L k is defined by Eq. ( 22). Therefore, the sought solutions (26) and (27) include, instead of the exponential functions, the new functions (85) dependent on the complex argument (iz/L k ).

div.head: VII. CONCLUSION
div.p: The paper has demonstrated how to derive the explicit analytic form for the current-voltage characteristics of the PN-junctions with nonuniformity in the cross section and doping impurity distribution by applying the transverse averaging technique (TAT). Application of the TAT to the three-dimensional transport equations of semiconductor electronics has converted them into the quasi-1D diffusion equations ( 13) and ( 14) to analyze the minority-carrier transport processes in the nonuniform PN-junctions.

Application of the spectral approach to the quasi-1D diffusion transport equations for the nonuniform PN-junctions under the action of arbitrary signal amplitude V ∼ has given rise to changes in both the static current-voltage characteristic J 0 (V 0 ) and the dynamic characteristics -the diffusion conductance G d (ω) and capacitance C d (ω), as compared with the conventional theory of uniform junctions [8]. These changes are caused by both factors -the signal amplitude V ∼ and the nonuniformity of S(z).

The large-signal effects on the static and dynamic characteristics have proved to be completely identical to those obtained theoretically and corroborated experimentally for the uniform PN-junctions in our previous papers [5,6]. As a next step, these results should be compared to simulation.

The influence of the cross-sectional nonuniformity on the static current-voltage characteristic (53) is exhibited in terms of the saturation current (54). The similar influence on the diffusion conductance G d (ω) and capacitance C d (ω) is demonstrated by the novel formulas (68)-( 69) and ( 77)-(78). The numerical calculations have been made for the exponential approximation S(z) = S 0 exp(2αz) of the cross-sectional z-dependence.

Until now, large-signal and high-frequency were treated separately to the best of our knowledge. This study may also have an impact in the understanding of distortion in high frequency circuits. Further applications, limitations of the model, carrier storage effects are the focus of further work.

figure.head: FIG. 1 :
figure.figDesc: FIG.1: Axially symmetric PN-junction; the depletion layer is situated between the cross sections z = -Wp of area Sp and z = Wn of area Sn.
0) e 2αWn ≡ (A α p + αL p ) e 2αLp(Wn/Lp) ,

figure.head: FIG. 3 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3: Low-frequency values of the factors F α G (0) (solid curves) and F α C (0) (dashed curves) versus the nonuniformity parameter αLp for three values of the ratio Wn/Lp = 0 (curves 1), 0.05 (curves 2), 0.1 (curves 3). The left and right inserts qualitatively show the longitudinal geometry of a nonuniform structure with α < 0 and α > 0.

div.head: Acknowledgment
div.p: One of the authors, AAB, thanks CNPq for the support during his stay at UFPE.
